segments.s270-g105: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when,” introducing a subordinate clause. گفتار پیران is a possessive/nominal phrase “the speech/words of the elders” (pīrān follows گفتار in the poetic style instead of using an explicit ezāfe). از آن سان is a prepositional phrase “in/from that manner/so,” and شنید is the simple past “he heard,” so the whole segment reads “when he heard the elders’ words spoken in that way.”
segments.s270-g106: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark سپه (the army/host) as the definite object. همه modifies سپه as “the whole/entire (host).” پای برجای is an idiomatic phrase (literally “foot in place”) meaning “standing firm/remaining in their places.” دید is the past tense verb “he saw,” with the subject omitted (understood from context to be the same agent as in the subordinate clause).
segments.s270-g73: بترسم is a 1st‑person present/fearive verb meaning “I fear.” همی is a preservative/progressive particle (classical Persian) that colors the verb as ongoing/emphatic (“I am/ever fearing”). زانک is a conjunction meaning “because/for,” and گردان سپهر is a noun phrase in which گردان is an adjective/participle “turning/revolving” modifying سپهر “the heavens/sky.” The whole segment gives the speaker’s fear and its reason (the revolving heavens).
segments.s270-g74: بخواهد is 3rd‑person singular present/future auxiliary meaning “(he/it) will/wishes to,” introducing an intended action. بریدن here means “to sever / break off (relations/affection).” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” with ما “us/we” as the governed pronoun. پاک modifies مهر as “pure,” and مهر is “love/affection.” Together this clause states the feared action: the heavens may wish to sever pure affection from us.
segments.s270-g75: وز آن پس is a classical sequence marker meaning “and/after that” (وز here is an archaic connective). شنیدم is 1st‑person past “I heard/learned.” یکی functions as an indefinite “one / a certain” (here “a certain person”), and بدخبر describes that person as ill‑omened or a bringer of bad news. The clause reports what the speaker heard next.
segments.s270-g76: کز آن is a subordinating phrase “from/for that / because of that” introducing consequence. نیز means “also / too,” and برگشتم is 1st‑person past “I returned / I turned back.” آسیمه سر is an idiomatic compound meaning “distraught / headlong / in a fluster,” where آسیمه is the adjective and سر reinforces the headlong sense. The clause expresses the speaker’s reaction to the news.
segments.s270-g77: که introduces a reported content clause “that …”. کیخسرو is the proper name “Kay Khosrow.” آید is 3rd‑person singular present/future “will come / comes.” همی again is the progressive/emphatic particle. با سپاه is a prepositional phrase “with an army,” where با governs سپاه (“army/host”). The clause gives the substance of the reported information.
segments.s270-g78: به پشت سپهبد is a locative phrase: به + پشت (“to/at the rear/behind”) with سپهبد (spahbed, a high commander) in the genitive relation — “to/at the rear of the spahbed.” بدین رزمگاه is an archaism meaning “to this battlefield” (بدین = به + این). Note the line’s older morphology: the preposition and demonstrative combine to mark direction/place (“to this field”).
segments.s270-g79: گر is the conditional particle “if.” ایدونک is an emphatic/particle (classical/poetic) used to reinforce “indeed/so” (here marking the reported rumor). گردد is a 3rd‑person subjunctive/present of گردیدن meaning “become / turn out / happen,” so the clause reads “if this report becomes/turns out to be true.” درست modifies خبر as “true/correct,” and این خبر = “this report/news.”
segments.s270-g80: که introduces the content of the report “that ….” خسرو here refers to the king/Khosrow. کند is the 3rd‑person present/future auxiliary “will/do” used with آمدن implied earlier (“will come”). سوی ما is the directional phrase “toward us,” and بر گذر is a locative phrase “at/on the pass” (گذر = pass/checkpoint). The clause states the feared movement toward the speaker’s position.
segments.s270-g57: This noun phrase names the affected thing: دل (the heart/seat of feeling) + a genitive-like modifier نامداران همه (the renowned ones, with همه quantifying 'all'). The prepositional/relational phrase بر شکست follows the noun: بر here marks the relation 'upon/at' with شکست 'defeat', so the whole segment says literally 'the hearts of all the famed (were) upon/at defeat' (i.e. their hearts were broken by the defeat). Word order puts the possessor/descriptor (نامداران همه) immediately after دل, which is typical in poetry.
segments.s270-g58: This clause states the result: همه شادمانی شد uses شادمانی 'joy/rejoicing' as the subject and شد 'became' as the past/predicate auxiliary meaning 'turned into'. از marks cause/reason ('because of'), and درد پست is a compound where درد is 'suffering/grief' and پست qualifies it as 'low/abject' (i.e. abject sorrow). The overall sense: 'all rejoicing became abject sorrow because of (their) grief.'
segments.s270-g59: و دیگر چو introduces a temporal clause: و دیگر 'and then/afterwards' (دیگر here functions adverbially) + چو 'when'. نستیهن نامدار is a proper name (Nastihan) qualified by نامدار 'renowned'. The clause thus sets the time: 'and then, when the famed Nastihan...'.
segments.s270-g60: ابا here is the classical verb/noun meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse' and is used intransitively; it reports hesitation/refusal. The following noun phrase ده هزار آزموده سوار names 'ten thousand proven/experienced horsemen' (آزموده modifying سوار). In context the line links Nastihan's hesitation with these seasoned horsemen — the sequence is compact, typical of epic diction, so the verb + numeral phrase form a terse narrative clause.
segments.s270-g61: برفت is an archaic/perfective verb meaning 'went away / departed' (here used impersonally of 'dawn'); the verb is placed before the following prepositional phrase. از بر من literally 'from off me / from my presence' (از = 'from', بر used relationally 'off/from [one's] side', من = 'me'). سپیده دمان is a poetic compound for 'the breaking of dawn' (سپیده = dawn, دمان = the rim/edge of the day). The clause thus reads 'the dawn departed from my side.'
segments.s270-g62: همان here = 'that same/at that very' (anaphoric intensifier). بیژنش is the proper name بیژن (Bijan) with the clitic -ش (his/it) attached in the manuscript tradition; functionally it points to Bijan as the actor. کند is the classical narrative verb (کند used as a past/hegemonic narrative 'did/raised'); سر 'head' is the direct object, در زمان 'at the time' marks the moment. Together: 'at that very moment Bijan raised his head.' The verb keeps the compact epic-style order: intensifier + actor + verb + object + temporal phrase.
segments.s270-g63: This first clause is first-person narrative: من 'I' + از درد دل 'from the pain/anguish of the heart' (از = 'out of/because of', درد دل = 'heart's pain' = grief) sets motive; برکشیدم is a past transitive verb 'I raised/lifted' (here used figuratively 'I mustered/raised (an army)'). سپاه 'army' is the direct object. The verb-final phrase is compact: motive + action + object.
segments.s270-g64: غریوان (collar/neckline, used metonymically for the bosom or 'with the collar/bosom disordered') functions as an adverbial or descriptive preface. برفتم is the first-person past 'I went/ I set out' (poetic past form), followed by به آوردگاه 'to the battlefield' (به = 'to/toward', آوردگاه = 'battlefield'). The sequence gives manner + verb + goal: 'with my collar disordered I went to the battlefield.'
segments.s270-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a 3rd-person singular past tense verb 'he heard'. گفتار پیران is a genitive noun phrase 'the speech/words of the elders' (noun + possessor after it). به درد is a short prepositional phrase 'in/with pain' (indicating that the elders' words were painful or caused sorrow) and attaches to the content of what was heard.
segments.s270-g98: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessive ش). گشت is the past/copular verb 'became' and governs the following predicate(s). پرخون is an adjectival/participial predicate 'blood-filled, bloodied' describing the heart. و links the two predicates; رخساره زرد is a verbless nominal phrase where رخساره 'complexion/face' is described by the adjective زرد 'pale' — the copula گشت scopes over both predicates.
segments.s270-g99: شد here is the past copula 'became'. از کار introduces the cause ('because of the affair/plight'). آن کشتگان خسته‌دل is a noun phrase 'those slain, (who were) heart-weary' giving the source of the distress; the whole phrase means 'he became (so) because of the fate/affair of those slain.'
segments.s270-g100: بدان is the prepositional unit 'به + آن' (= 'to/into that'); here it means 'to that (sorrow)'. درد names the sorrow/pain. بنهاد is a past verb meaning 'placed/put' (he set or gave). پیوسته is an adverb 'continually/constantly'. دل 'heart' is the object: 'he continually set/gave his heart to that sorrow.' Note: the segmentation in the word list separates ب and دان; دان does not correspond to its usual senses here and is best read as the demonstrative 'آن'.
segments.s270-g101: وز آن نیز is an archaic sequence equivalent to 'و از آن نیز' meaning 'and also from that'. کز (ک + از) introduces the following clause with a causal/relational sense 'that/from which' (i.e. 'from which it happened that...'). دشمنان لشکرش is 'the enemies (and) his army' with لشکرش 'his army' bearing the possessive; the clause sets up that his army fled from the enemies.
segments.s270-g102: گریزان is a participle 'fleeing/ having fled' (here indicating the army's flight). و is the conjunction 'and'. ویران‌شده is a perfect-participial adjective 'ruined/destroyed' and کشورش 'his country' is the noun it modifies; together: 'his army fled and his country was ruined.'
segments.s270-g103: ز هر سو is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every side). پلنگ is the attacking animal 'leopard/panther'. اندر آورده is a compound verb meaning 'have brought in/let out' — here 'have bared/revealed' — and چنگ 'claw' is the object: 'leopards from every side have brought/clawed (at him).' The order is typical poetic word order where the verb phrase follows the subject and adverbial.
segments.s270-g104: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'against/upon him'. The second بر before جهان is emphatic/poetic and links the following predicate to 'for him' — بر جهان گشته تاریک و تنگ literally 'upon the world (it) became dark and narrow.' گشته here is the past participle/verb 'became/turned', and تاریک و تنگ are the resulting adjectival predicates 'dark and narrow' describing how the world seemed to him.
segments.s270-g41: نجستیم is the strong-past (perfect) first-person plural of جستیدن used here to mean 'we sought' (not 'we leapt'); it precedes its object رزم in a poetic V–O order. اندر آن کینه‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in/within that seat/den of hatred' following the object and specifying where the seeking took place.
segments.s270-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/whether') and آید is the present (or modal/potential) form 'may/comes'; مَگر here functions archaically/poetically to express doubt or 'perhaps' (not the listed senses exactly), so the clause reads like 'if/perhaps an army will come'. سوی هامون is a directional phrase 'toward the plain,' and سپاه (the actual subject of آید) is placed after the direction by poetic inversion.
segments.s270-g43: نیامد is the negative past 'did not come.' سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ‑ش 'his army' (the clitic attached directly). The sequence از آن‌که begins a causal clause (in classical Persian acting like 'because/for the fact that') and برون 'out/forth' begins that subordinate phrase; the whole segment is the negative main clause followed by the causal marker.
segments.s270-g44: سر پهلوانان ما is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the head(s) of our champions' (سر = anatomical 'head'). شد is the past of شدن used as 'became' and نگون is a predicative adjective 'overthrown/turned down' — 'the heads of our champions became overthrown' (a poetic way to say our champions were destroyed).
segments.s270-g45: سپهدار ایران is a titled noun phrase 'the commander of Iran.' نیامد is the past negative 'did not come.' ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary/exhausted' describing the subject (Persian often places the adjective after the verb without an explicit copula).
segments.s270-g46: به هامون is a destination phrase 'to the plain.' نیاورد is the past negative 'did not bring' (نیاوردن = fail to bring / did not bring). لشکر ز کوه is 'an army from the mountain,' where ز is the poetic form of از 'from.' The verb omits an explicit subject (it refers back to سپهدار ایران).
segments.s270-g47: The sequence places titles/epithets and possessive in a compact classical order: برادر جهاندار هومان من = 'Homan, the world‑ruler, my brother.' من is the emphatic/possessive pronoun 'my' attached after the name for emphasis; جهاندار is an epithet modifying Homan.
segments.s270-g48: بجوشید is the past tense of جوشیدن used metaphorically 'seethed/boiled' (with emotion). به کینه uses به to mark the state or manner 'in/with hatred.' از این انجمن gives the cause/source 'from/because of this assembly' (here از signals the reason). Together: 'he seethed with hatred because of this assembly.'
segments.s270-g1: از اسبان گله is a prepositional/genitive-like phrase meaning 'from the herd of horses' (literal word order: از + noun + noun). هرچ شایسته بود is a subordinate clause 'whatever was suitable'; شایسته is an adjective 'suitable/appropriate' and بود is the past-tense copula 'was'. Together they mean 'whatever horses in the herds were suitable.'
segments.s270-g2: ز هر سو 'from every side/direction' (ز + هر + سو). به لشکرگه آورد زود is the main action: آورد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular 'brought', به لشکرگه 'to the army-camp', and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' placed after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s270-g3: پیاده همه یعنی 'all who were on foot' (پیاده = 'on foot/infantry', همه = 'all'). کرد is the causative/past 'made/turned (them into)', and یکسر سوار is a result phrase 'wholly/entirely mounted' (یکسر = 'entirely', سوار = 'mounted' here). The structure is cause (کرد) with object (پیاده همه) and resultant predicate (یکسر سوار).
segments.s270-g4: دو اسبه سوار is a compound noun phrase 'two-horse riders' (دو quantifies اسبه 'horse/steed', together modifying سوار 'rider'). از پس کارزار is a temporal/sequence phrase 'after the battle' (از پس = 'in the wake of/after', کارزار = 'battle'). The verb is omitted here (poetic ellipsis) and the phrase describes who followed.
segments.s270-g5: سر گنجهای کهن is an idafa-like construction 'the top/heads of the ancient treasures' (سر = 'top/head', گنجهای = 'treasures', کهن = 'ancient'). برگشاد is a past participial/past verb 'opened' describing the action done to those treasure-vaults.
segments.s270-g6: به دینار دادن is a prepositional + verbal-noun phrase 'to/for giving dinars' (دادن here functions as the verbal noun 'giving'); دل اندر نهاد is a compact past clause 'he put his heart into (it)' where نهاد is past of نهادن 'placed', دل = 'heart' (object), and اندر = 'in/into'. The whole phrase expresses that he devoted himself to distributing money.
segments.s270-g7: چو این کرده شد is a temporal clause 'when this had been done' where چو = 'when', این = 'this', کرده شد is a compound passive/perfect 'was done'. نزد افراسیاب 'in the presence of Afrasiab' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of'). Word order is the usual poetic sequence: temporal clause + locative.
segments.s270-g8: نوندی is presented here as a proper name or rare term (subject) and برافگند is the past tense verb 'threw off / scattered / cast' (third-person singular). هنگام خواب is a temporal adverbial 'at the time of sleep' (هنگام = 'at the time of', خواب = 'sleep') modifying the verb. The clause therefore places the action by 'نوندی' at the time of sleep.
segments.s270-g17: نه زیبا بود (lit. 'was not beautiful') is a negated past/copular clause with بود (past of بودن). جز تو is an exception phrase ('except you') that attaches to the clause. مر تخت را is a topical/definite object ('my throne') where مر is the enclitic possessive 'my' and را marks the noun as definite/emphatic; the verb بود scopes over this whole phrase.
segments.s270-g18: کلاه و کمر بستن is a nominal/verbal-noun sequence ('the putting on / binding of the cap and the girdle'); بستن is the infinitive/masdar used to list actions. و links the next item; بخت را ('fortune' with را) is added to the list. The verb is not repeated here—the list is understood as completing what makes the throne notable (ellipsis of an overt verb is typical in poetry).
segments.s270-g19: از آن کس is a prepositional phrase 'from that person'. برآرد is a classical third-person verb meaning 'brings forth/raises' (here with an imperfective/optative sense). جهاندار گرد functions as a compound: جهاندار 'world-ruler' and گرد supplying a verbal/adjectival flavor ('become' / 'conqueror' in older usage), so the clause means 'would raise/make (from him) a world-ruler/leader.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s270-g20: که here introduces a relative clause ('who/that'), پیش تو آید means 'comes before you' (پیش = before/in the presence of, آید = comes; classical present/subjunctive form), and به‌روز نبرد is a time phrase 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'on the day', نبرد = 'battle'). Together it qualifies the person raised in the previous segment.
segments.s270-g21: یکی opens with a distributive/indefinite sense ('one, a certain'); بنده‌ام is 'I am a servant' with the first-person clitic -ام and functions as a humble self-reference. من is an emphatic pronoun reinforcing the speaker, and گنهکار تو places 'sinner' as description tied to the addressee's possession (i.e. 'your sinner/ sinful one') — overall: 'I am but one sinful servant of yours.'
segments.s270-g22: کشیده is a past participle 'drawn/pulled' (acting predicatively), modifying سر 'head'. از جان is an ablative phrase 'from (my) life/soul' and بیدار تو places the agent/recipient at the end—'you, the wakeful/vigilant one'. The whole clause uses the participle plus agent to say 'you have drawn my head from my life' (poetic/metaphorical expression).
segments.s270-g23: This line is inverted for emphasis: ز کیخسرو از من is a comparative-like construction ('more from Kay Khosrow than from me' / 'at Kay Khosrow rather than at me'), بیازرد (classical form) expresses 'be angered/offended' or 'take offense', and شاه at the end is the addressed subject 'O king' placed there for emphasis. Literal sense: 'O king, may you be angered at Kay Khosrow rather than at me.'
segments.s270-g24: جز این خویشتن را sets up the exception phrase 'except this self' (جز = except, این خویشتن = this self, را marks it as definite/emphatic). ندانم is the negative present/past classical 'I do not know / I know not' (here 'I know no...'), and گناه is the noun 'sin/fault'. Together: 'I know no sin except this self.'
segments.s270-g49: به ایران سپه شد is a past narrative clause: the prepositional phrase به ایران (towards/into Iran) precedes the verbal phrase سپه شد (“an army/host came/was set”). The conjunction/particle که introduces a subordinate purpose/relative clause (here 'in order to' / 'that') and جوید (present-form used poetically) expresses the seeking action with نبرد as its object (‘to seek battle’). Word order fronting the place and the use of که + present-form for purpose are typical of classical Persian epic diction.
segments.s270-g50: ندانم is the negated 1st-person present/past-stem idiom ‘I do not know’. چه introduces an indirect interrogative 'what', آمد here means 'happened' / 'befell' (rather than simple 'come'), and بر آن شیرمرد marks the target affected ('upon that brave man'). The clause is a parenthetical comment attached to the previous clause.
segments.s270-g51: بیامد is a simple past ‘came’. The sequence به کین جستنش is a purpose/infinitival phrase: به + کین (‘to/for vengeance’) followed by جستنش (the verbal noun + enclitic object ‘its seeking’), so the whole means 'came to seek/for vengeance'. پور گیو is a name-label ‘the son of Giv’ placed after the verbal phrase as subject/appositive in epic style.
segments.s270-g52: بگردید is a past/epic perfective meaning 'he went about / roamed / searched'. با introduces the accompanyer ('with'), گرد here functions as 'troop / company / circle' (used idiomatically with a leader's name), and هومان names that leader; نیو is an epithet/name following Homan. The whole clause describes the manner/companions of his searching.
segments.s270-g53: چون here functions temporally ('when/as'), introducing the time clause. ابر دست is a compound where ابر (literally 'cloud') modifies دست ('hand/power') to mean a great or overwhelming hand/power; بیژنی is a patronymic/epithet referring to Bijan and کشته شد is the passive perfect 'was killed/was slain'. The clause as a whole gives the temporal circumstance for the following result clause.
segments.s270-g54: The result clause has سر من ('my head' = metonymically 'my life/fate') as topic, ز تیمار او ('from his care') marking source/cause with the poetic preposition ز, and گشته شد a compound perfect-passive-like construction (گشته 'turned/been made' + شد as auxiliary) meaning 'was taken/was rendered'. The sequence indicates that, as a consequence of the killing, the speaker's fate was separated from that person's protection.
segments.s270-g55: This is a rhetorical clause: که دانست هرگز 'who ever knew' (که introduces the interrogative/relative, دانست 'knew', هرگز used rhetorically as 'ever/at any time'), followed by another که introducing the content clause. سرو بلند ('the tall cypress') is the subject/topic placed at the end of the segment in typical poetic inversion.
segments.s270-g56: به باغ 'in/to the garden' is a locative phrase; از گیا ('from the vegetation/plants') marks the source or cause of the forthcoming misfortune. یافت خواهد گزند is a periphrastic future: یافت ('find/encounter') together with خواهد ('will') plus گزند ('harm') yields 'will encounter harm' (i.e. 'will be harmed'). The verb cluster thus expresses a future passive-like event affecting the cypress.
segments.s270-g89: This is a compact temporal clause: 'فرستاده' (the messenger) + 'چون' (here functioning as a temporal 'when') + 'گفت' (spoke/said) + 'پیران شنید' (the elders heard). Word order places the time/clause ('فرستاده چون گفت') before the main verb 'شنید'; the understood sense is 'When the messenger spoke, the elders listened.'
segments.s270-g90: 'به کردار' is an adverbial simile marker 'in the manner of / like'; it modifies the verb that follows. 'باد' is literal 'wind' and 'دمان' (hem/skirt) is the object that 'بردمید' (past, transitive 'tore/lifted/took away') acts on. Grammatically the elders (subject carried over from the previous clause) are understood to perform the action — 'they, like the wind, lifted/tore up their hems.'
segments.s270-g91: 'نشست' is a past verb meaning 'sat down/ alighted' here (dismounted). The compound prepositional phrase 'از بر' = 'from off (the back of)'; syntactically the verb is followed by the source phrase 'از بر بادپای سمند' ('from the back of the swift-footed steed').
segments.s270-g92: 'به کردار' again introduces a simile ('in the manner of'). 'آتش' ('fire') and the noun phrase 'هیونی بلند' ('a tall beast') form the image: the steed (or the scene) is compared to a towering, fiery beast. The clause is elliptical — the simile modifies the prior action and uses adjective 'بلند' postpositively to qualify 'هیونی'.
segments.s270-g93: 'بشد' is an impersonal/past narrative marker meaning 'it came to pass / he came' (here translated as 'he came'). 'تا' introduces the extent/limit ('until/to'), followed by the prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک افراسیاب' ('to/near Afrasiab'). Together they mean 'he advanced until he reached near Afrasiab.'
segments.s270-g94: This is a triadic negative: 'نه ... نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'نه دم زد' literally 'did not (even) draw a moment/breath' (i.e. did not stop even for a moment); 'به ره بر' = 'on the road'; the second disjunct 'نه آرام و خواب' lists 'rest / sleep' as things he did not do. The verbs are past, and omission of an explicit subject carries it from the previous clause.
segments.s270-g95: 'به نزدیک شاه اندر آمد' is a simple motion clause: 'came to the vicinity of the king' (preposition 'به' + noun 'نزدیک شاه' + verb 'اندر آمد' = 'entered/approached'). 'چو باد' is a simile 'as/like the wind' modifying the manner of his coming; here 'چو' functions as 'as/when' used comparatively.
segments.s270-g96: Two coordinated past actions: 'ببوسید تخت' — 'he kissed the throne' (direct object 'تخت'), then 'و پیامش بداد' — 'and he gave/delivered his message.' 'پیامش' is the object with the possessive clitic '-ش' = 'his'. The verb 'بداد' here functions as the past of 'دادن' ('gave/delivered') in older usage.
segments.s270-g33: This is a VSO order common in classical Persian poetry: the (contractional) prepositional phrase comes first, then the past tense verb, then the noun phrase subject. "وز" here is the poetic contraction of و + ز (and/from) meaning "and from"; "آن‌سو" is the place phrase "that side/over there"; "بیامد" is simple past third-person singular "came"; "سپاهی گران" is a noun + adjective phrase "a heavy/mighty army" (adjective after noun).
segments.s270-g34: A noun phrase naming the leader followed by an ellipsis (no verb) and a prepositional phrase. "سپهدار گودرز" is an apposition/title + proper name ("the commander Goudarz"); the conjunction "و" links the next phrase; "با او سران" literally "with him (were) the chiefs," where the copula/verb is omitted (typical in poetry) and "با" governs the pronoun "او" and the noun "سران" (leaders).
segments.s270-g35: "کز ... ز گاه ... شاه" introduces a temporal clause meaning 'since the time of ... the king'. Here "کز" functions as the archaic/poetic 'from/that from' (introducing the clause), "ایران" names the land, "ز" is the poetic preposition 'from', "گاه" means 'time' or 'days', and "منوچهر شاه" is the proper name plus title 'King Manuchehr.' Word order is compact and somewhat inverted because of poetry.
segments.s270-g36: Main clause: "فزون زان نیامد به توران سپاه" = 'no army greater than that came to Turan.' "فزون زان" is a comparative phrase 'greater/more than that' (فزون = more/more numerous; زان = 'than that'); "نیامد" is past negative 'did not come'; "به توران" is the destination prepositional phrase 'to Turan'; "سپاه" (army) appears post-verb as the subject/topic (verb–subject order is normal in older poetry).
segments.s270-g37: "به زیبد" is an idiomatic/predicative phrase meaning 'it was fitting' or 'suitable' (here functioning adverbially), followed by the object "یکی جایگه" ('one place' = 'a single spot/camp'). "ساختند" is the past tense plural 'they made/built.' The clause thus means 'they made a fitting/suitable place.'
segments.s270-g38: A transitive clause with topicalized object: "سپه را" (the army, marked by the object/topic marker "را"), followed by the locative phrase "در آن کوه" ('in that mountain'), and the verb "بنشاختند" at the end: past plural 'they set up/they stationed.' The construction means 'they stationed the army in that mountain.'
segments.s270-g39: Duration and simile placed after the topicalized object: "سپه را" (the army), then the duration "سه روز و سه شب" ('three days and three nights'), and the simile "چون پلنگ" ('like a leopard'). The sequence emphasizes how long and in what manner the army was held—the simile modifies their behavior/stance.
segments.s270-g40: "به روی اندر آورده" is a verbal phrase built from the verb "آوردن" (to bring) with the locative/face noun "روی" and the preposition "اندر" ('into/on'), giving 'brought forth/put on display' or 'brought to the front.' "بد روی تنگ" is a descriptive cluster: "بد روی" = 'ill-faced' / 'grim-faced' and "تنگ" = 'tight/strained' (faces drawn tight). The clause is therefore pictorial: they brought them out with grim, contracted faces. Note the adjective phrases follow the nouns as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s270-g65: This clause sets the scene: 'یکی' (indefinite 'one / a certain') modifies 'رزم' (an engagement/clash). The temporal phrase 'تا شب' ('until night') attaches to the verb 'برآمد' (literally 'arose/appeared / came forth'), and 'ز کوه' ('from the mountain') indicates the source/place from which the engagement came. Word order is noun + temporal phrase + verb + source, common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s270-g66: 'بکردیم' is past tense, 1st-person-plural ('we did/made'); here it means 'we joined / made (one)'. The preposition 'با' ('with') + 'دگر' ('other, another') and the compound noun 'همگروه' ('fellow-member, comrade') together indicate reciprocal joining: 'we made one with another (as comrades)'. The prefix 'ب-' plus past stem is an older/poetic formation but functions like regular past tense.
segments.s270-g67: The sentence begins with the conjunction 'چو' used temporally as 'when'. 'نهصد تن' ('nine hundred persons') is the subject, followed by 'از نامداران شاه' ('of the king's renowned men') which is a genitive phrase specifying whose men they were. This is standard Persian noun-modifier order (number + noun + prepositional/genitive modifier).
segments.s270-g68: 'سر از تن جدا شد' is a semi-idiomatic passive/resultado expression literally 'the head became separate from the body' — i.e. they were decapitated. 'شد' is a past (perfective) verb 'became' used to mark the change of state. 'بر این رزمگاه' ('on this battlefield') is a locative prepositional phrase placed after the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s270-g69: 'دو بهره' literally 'two portions/shares' functions here as 'two (men)'—a poetic way to count members. 'ز گردان این انجمن' ('from the troop of this assembly/company') is a genitive chain: 'from (the) troop' + modifier 'this assembly'. The preposition 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from/of'.
segments.s270-g70: This is a compact descriptive clause: 'دل از درد خسته' = 'heart(s) tired from pain' (an adjectival phrase where 'دل' is the affected organ and 'از درد' gives cause). 'به شمشیر تن' uses 'به' instrumentally ('by/with') to indicate the body was dealt with by the sword — elliptical: 'their bodies (were) by the sword' meaning struck or cut by the sword. The clause omits a copula/auxiliary explicitly because of the poetic, telegraphic style.
segments.s270-g71: 'به ما بر شده' is an impersonal/experience construction meaning 'it has befallen us' or 'has come upon us'; here it introduces the result. 'چیره ایرانیان' follows and names the agent/state: 'the Iranians (are) dominant / have prevailed'. 'شده' (become/happened) and 'چیره' (dominant/prevailing) together express that the Iranians have prevailed over us.
segments.s270-g72: This clause explains how they prevail: 'به کینه' ('with hatred') marks the manner, 'همه' ('all/everyone') is the scope, and 'پاک بسته میان' is a poetic verb phrase 'have cleanly/utterly tied/bound (them) between' — i.e. 'they have tied everyone together in complete hatred'. 'پاک' here is adverbial ('utterly/completely') modifying 'بسته' ('tied/bound'), and 'میان' ('between') indicates the relational bond among people.
segments.s270-g9: فرستاده‌ای is a noun formed from the verb stem (literally 'one sent') used here as an indefinite subject, 'a messenger' or 'a certain envoy'. با is the preposition 'with' introducing accompanying materials; هش و رای is a coordinated noun phrase 'warning (هش) and counsel (رای)', and پیر postmodifies those nouns (the old man whose warning and counsel they are), a common poetic order where the epithet follows the nouns it qualifies.
segments.s270-g10: A simple coordinated list of persons accompanying the messenger: سخن‌گوی 'a spokesman/speaker', گرد here functions as an escort/retinue (poetic/archaic usage) and is joined by و to سوار 'riders/horsemen', and then و دبیر 'and a scribe/secretary'. The coordination (و) strings nouns together; there is no finite verb in this nominal list because it continues the description begun in the previous segment.
segments.s270-g11: که introduces an indirect command/purpose clause ('that / so that'); رو is an archaic verb-form meaning 'go' (imperative/subjunctive), followed by the destination شاه توران 'the Shah/king of Turan'. سپه را is the object 'the army/host' with the definite object marker را, and بگوی is the archaic subjunctive/imperative form of 'say/tell' governing the reported words. Word order is typical classical Persian where the verb (imperative/subjunctive) appears after its objects.
segments.s270-g12: که‌ای here introduces the quoted direct address that the envoy is to deliver; the phrase functions like 'saying: "O ..."' where ای is the vocative 'O' attached to the following epithet. دادگر is an epithet 'just' modifying خسرو (the name Khosrow), and نامجوی is a descriptive epithet 'seeker of a name/fame' (a compound adjective used poetically).
segments.s270-g13: کز آنگه که is a fixed temporal phrase 'from that time when' (کز = 'from/that', آنگه = 'then', که = relative 'that/when'). چرخ سپهر is a poetic tautology for 'the heavens/firmament' (two synonymous nouns), and بلند is an adjective 'lofty/high' modifying them.
segments.s270-g14: بگشت is an archaic past verb meaning 'turned/returned/was turned' (here 'turned aside'); از بر is a prepositional combination indicating separation or movement away from ('from/off' the surface of); تیره خاک نژند is a nominal phrase 'the dark, base earth' where تیره 'dark/ominous' and خاک 'earth/soil' are followed by the adjective نژند 'base/worthless' describing the earth.
segments.s270-g15: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'; تو is the subject 'you' with شاه as an appositive or title 'king'; بر گاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the seat/throne' (گاه used poetically as 'seat/occasion'), and ننشست is the past negative 'did not sit' (postalgebraic order places the verb after its complements). نیز 'also/likewise' links to the following clause.
segments.s270-g16: به کس here means 'to anyone' (indirect recipient); نام شاهی is 'the name/title of kingship' (نام = 'name/title', شاهی = 'kingship/royal status'); نپیوست is the negative past of پیوستن 'to attach/join' meaning 'did not attach' or idiomatically 'did not become attached to anyone' — i.e., 'no one became king.' The final نیز repeats 'also', emphasizing the same negation.
segments.s270-g81: جهاندار داند که من با سپاه is a clause with Subject–Verb–complement order typical of classical Persian: جهاندار (subject, 'the sovereign') + داند (3rd person singular present or narrative 'knows') + که (subordinating 'that') introducing the content clause. من با سپاه ('I with an army') is the topic/agent phrase of the subordinate clause and appears before the verb that completes that clause.
segments.s270-g82: نیارم شدن پیش او کینه‌خواه is the predicate of the content clause introduced by که. شدن is the infinitive/auxiliary 'to become' (here forming a verbal phrase), پیش او 'before him/in his presence' specifies the spatial relation, and کینه‌خواه is the noun/adjective 'avenger' or 'vengeful (one)' that describes the role. نیارم is not used here as one of its lexical senses listed (it functions idiomatically/verbally in this line), so its gloss is given as an idiomatic element meaning roughly 'to act/come (in such a role)'; the whole phrase therefore reads 'to become/appear before him as an avenger.'
segments.s270-g83: مگر شاه با لشکر کینه‌جوی opens a conditional/exception clause: مگر 'unless' introduces the condition; شاه is the subject 'the king'; با لشکر 'with an army' is an instrumental phrase, and کینه‌جوی is an adjectival modifier 'vengeful/revenge-seeking' that qualifies لشکر.
segments.s270-g84: نهد سوی ایران بدین کینه روی: نهد is the 3rd-person singular present/narrative of نهاد used as a verb 'to place/set/dispatch' ('sets out' here). سوی ایران is the directional complement 'toward Iran'. بدین کینه روی should be read as بدین (به + این, 'with this') + کینه 'grudge' + روی 'face/impetus' — a poetic way to say 'with this grudge/with this grudge-filled purpose.' Note that the tokenizer has split بدین into ب + دین; دین as listed (religion) does not fit here, so treat ب + دین together as the prepositional phrase 'بدین' = 'with this.'
segments.s270-g85: بگرداند این بد ز تورانیان begins with بگرداند (3rd-person singular verb 'he turns/causes to turn' or 'he directs'), verb-initial for emphasis as often in poetic prose. این بد 'this evil' is the object/theme, and ز تورانیان ('from/against the Turanians') marks the source/target depending on context; here it reads as the object being directed at the Turanians (poetic agency).
segments.s270-g86: ببندد به کینه کمر بر میان is a coordinated verbal phrase: ببندد (3rd-person singular 'he binds/girds') takes به کینه 'with hatred' as the instrument/content; کمر 'waist/loins' is the body-part object that is being girded (کمر به کینه ببندد = 'gird his loins with hatred'), and بر میان 'upon the middle' is a locative/emphatic phrase meaning 'to enter the midst' or 'for the fray.' The construction is idiomatic for preparing oneself eagerly or wrathfully for combat.
segments.s270-g87: که‌گر جان ما را ز ایران سپاه is the protasis (if-clause): که‌گر (archaic conditional 'if') introduces the condition; جان ما را places the direct object 'our life(s)' before the rest of the clause (object marked by را); ز ایران سپاه is the source phrase 'an army from Iran.' Word order places the object before the agent phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s270-g88: بد آید نباشد کسی کینه‌خواه: بد آید ('evil/harm comes' — آید is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of آمدن) states the adverse event. نباشد is a negative existential/optative ('there will not be' / 'no one will be [otherwise]') and in this context combines with کسی 'anyone' and predicate کینه‌خواه 'an avenger' to mean 'there will be no one who is not an avenger' or more naturally 'there will be no one who will not take vengeance' — i.e., everyone will become vengeful. Note that نباشد here functions idiomatically to produce the universal negative.
segments.s270-g25: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). این ایزدی بود is a simple predication: این (this) + ایزدی (predicate, 'divine') + بود (past 'was'). The following verblike repetition بود آنچ بود ('was that which it was') is a poetic, emphatic formula: آنچ ('that which') followed by بود repeats and reinforces the state; word order is flexible and elliptical for effect.
segments.s270-g26: ندارد is a negative existential/possessive verb ('does not have' / 'there is not'). The construction places the verb first for emphasis, followed by the source preposition ز ('from/ because of') + گفتار ('speech') + modifier بسیار ('much'). سود ('benefit') is the governed noun; overall: 'there is no much/real benefit from speech.'
segments.s270-g27: اگر is the conditional 'if'; نیز ('also') adds emphasis. بیند is a present/subjunctive-form verb used here in a conditional sense ('sees' / 'should see'). مرا is the object 'me' (the analyzer split it into م + را), and ز این گناه is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from this sin' giving the reason or charge.
segments.s270-g28: کند is a general 3rd-person verb form here ('he will do / will make'); it takes گردن ('neck') as its direct object (understood to refer back to مرا) and آزد/آزاد ('free') as the resultative adjective — 'he will set (my) neck free' i.e. 'he will release/spare me.' و آید به راه is coordinate: آید ('he will come/go') + به راه ('on his way') = 'and he will go on his way.'
segments.s270-g29: رسانم is first-person verb 'I convey / I will bring (news)' placed before the explicit subject من (poetic V–S order). اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. به شاه آگهی is a dative phrase: به شاه ('to the king') + آگهی ('news/information'), indicating the recipient and the content of the message.
segments.s270-g30: که introduces the content clause ('what ...'). گردون is the subject here used as 'fate/destiny' rather than literally 'sky'. چه is the interrogative/relative 'what', and آورد is past 'brought'. پیش رهی ('before / in the presence of the wayfarer') marks the beneficiary/scene — together: 'what fate has brought before the wayfarer.'
segments.s270-g31: کشیدم is first-person past 'I drew/moved' (‑م marks 'I'). The verb is followed by the directional phrase به کوه ('to the mountain'). کنابد سپاه is a poetic inversion: کنابد ('suffers/toils') is the verb describing سپاه ('the army/host'); subject follows verb for narrative effect.
segments.s270-g32: به ایرانیان marks the target/party (literally 'to/against the Iranians'). بر is the adverbial/locational/against-marker combined with the verb ببستیم ('we shut/closed' — first-person plural past), and راه ('road') is the object: 'we closed the road against the Iranians' (i.e. blocked their passage).

sentences.s270-s53: When he heard the elders' words put in that way, he saw the whole army standing fast in their places.
sentences.s270-s37: I fear, for the turning heavens may wish to sever pure affection from us.
sentences.s270-s38: After that I heard from a bearer of bad news; because of that I too turned back, distraught.
sentences.s270-s39: That Kay Khosrow will come with an army to the rear of the spahbed at this battlefield.
sentences.s270-s40: If indeed this report proves true — that Khosrow will come toward us at the pass.
sentences.s270-s29: The hearts of all the famed were broken; all rejoicing turned into abject sorrow from deep anguish.
sentences.s270-s30: And then, when the renowned Nastihan hesitated, ten thousand proven horsemen (were involved).
sentences.s270-s31: Dawn departed from before me; at that very moment Bijan lifted his head.
sentences.s270-s32: Out of the anguish of my heart I mustered an army; with my collar disordered I went to the battlefield.
sentences.s270-s49: When he heard the elders' words (that caused sorrow), his heart became full of blood and his face turned pale.
sentences.s270-s50: He became, from the plight of those slain, weary of heart; to that sorrow he continually gave his heart.
sentences.s270-s51: And moreover, from that (trouble) his army fled from the enemies, and his country was laid waste.
sentences.s270-s52: From every side leopards drew forth their claws against him; for him the world had become dark and narrow.
sentences.s270-s21: We sought battle within that den of hatred, wondering whether a host might come toward the plain.
sentences.s270-s22: His army did not come forth, for the heads of our champions were cast down.
sentences.s270-s23: The commander of Iran did not come, exhausted; he did not bring an army down from the mountain to the plain.
sentences.s270-s24: My brother Homan, the world-ruler, seethed with hatred because of this assembly.
sentences.s270-s1: He quickly brought to the camp, from every direction, whatever suitable horses there were in the herds.
sentences.s270-s2: He made all the infantry entirely mounted; two-horse riders followed after the fighting.
sentences.s270-s3: He opened the tops (lids/vaults) of the ancient treasuries and set his heart to giving dinars.
sentences.s270-s4: When this had been done before Afrasiab, Nundi threw off (cast/dispersed) at the time of sleep.
sentences.s270-s9: My throne was not beautiful except for you — the putting on of the cap and the girding of the waist, and (the) fortune.
sentences.s270-s10: From that man the world-ruler would raise (or make) a lordly figure who will come before you on the day of battle.
sentences.s270-s11: I am but one of your servants, sinful; you, vigilant one, have drawn my head from my life.
sentences.s270-s12: May the king be angered at Kay Khosrow rather than at me; I know of no sin except this self.
sentences.s270-s25: The host went into Iran to seek battle; I do not know what befell that brave man.
sentences.s270-s26: Giv’s son came to avenge it; he went about searching with Homan’s troop.
sentences.s270-s27: When Bijan was slain by a mighty hand, my head (my life) was removed from his care.
sentences.s270-s28: Who could ever have known that the tall cypress in the garden would meet with harm from the plants?
sentences.s270-s45: When the messenger spoke, the elders heard; like the wind they lifted their hems.
sentences.s270-s46: He alighted from the back of the swift steed; like a tall, fiery beast it reared up.
sentences.s270-s47: He came up near Afrasiab; he neither paused for a moment on the road nor rested or slept.
sentences.s270-s48: He came near the king like the wind; he kissed the throne and delivered his message.
sentences.s270-s17: From that side came a mighty army; the commander Goudarz, and with him the chiefs.
sentences.s270-s18: Since the days of King Manuchehr, no army greater than that has come from Iran to Turan.
sentences.s270-s19: They made a single suitable camp, and they stationed the army in that mountain.
sentences.s270-s20: They kept the army for three days and three nights like a leopard, bringing them forth with grim, drawn faces.
sentences.s270-s33: One engagement rose from the mountain and continued until night; we joined together, one with another, as comrades.
sentences.s270-s34: When nine hundred of the king's famed men were cut off from their bodies on this battlefield.
sentences.s270-s35: Two men of this troop — their hearts weary from pain, their bodies struck by the sword.
sentences.s270-s36: The Iranians gained the upper hand over us; they have tied everyone together in complete hatred.
sentences.s270-s5: He sent a messenger with the old man's warning and counsel — a spokesman, an escort of horsemen, and a scribe.
sentences.s270-s6: to go to the Shah of Turan and tell (him) of the host, saying, 'O just Khosrow, seeker of a name.'
sentences.s270-s7: From that time when the lofty sky and firmament turned aside from the dark, base earth.
sentences.s270-s8: When you did not sit upon the throne, likewise no one acquired the name (title) of kingship.
sentences.s270-s41: The sovereign knows that I will not, with an army, go before him as an avenger.
sentences.s270-s42: Unless the king, with a vengeful army, sets out toward Iran with this grudge in mind.
sentences.s270-s43: He will direct this evil at the Turanians and will gird his loins with hatred for battle.
sentences.s270-s44: If an army from Iran should bring harm to our lives, there will be no one who will not become an avenger.
sentences.s270-s13: For this was divine; it was what it was, and much speech brings no benefit.
sentences.s270-s14: If he sees me for this sin, he will free me and go on his way.
sentences.s270-s15: I will now bring news to the king of what fate has brought before the wayfarer.
sentences.s270-s16: I drew the host to the mountain; the army labored, and we closed the road against the Iranians.
